summary:
This week's research highlights a strong focus on enhancing the reasoning capabilities and reliability of AI agents and Large Language Models (LLMs).

**Popular Directions:**

*   **Agentic Systems and Lifecycle Automation:** AutoSci exemplifies a push towards comprehensive agentic systems that manage the entire scientific research lifecycle, emphasizing memory and iterative improvement.
*   **LLM Reasoning Deep Dives:** Several papers tackle improving LLM reasoning through structured search histories (LinTree), long-context learning with granular rewards (LongTraceRL), and understanding underlying mechanisms like positional vs. symbolic attention (Positional versus Symbolic Attention Heads).
*   **Multimodal AI and Evaluation:** A significant theme is the development and evaluation of multimodal models, particularly in computer vision and video comprehension (Vision-Language Models Suppress Female Representations, Moment-Video). This includes adapting models for specific domains like oncology (When Are Multimodal Predictions Biologically Supported?).
*   **Continual Learning and Adaptation:** Researchers are exploring how agents and models can learn continuously and adapt to new tasks without forgetting previous knowledge, seen in frameworks like AgentCL and advanced MoE training systems like PithTrain and CRAM.
*   **Robustness and Reliability:** A crucial trend is the drive for more reliable AI, addressing issues like factual density in RAG (Evaluating Factual Density), hallucination evaluation (BenHalluEval), and ethical safety, notably concerning eating disorder queries (Food Noise & False Safety).

**Notable Advances:**

*   **Memory-Centric AI:** AutoSci's memory system (SciMem) represents a notable advance in structured knowledge management for research automation.
*   **Personalized Evaluation:** PARL offers a novel approach to learn personalized evaluation rubrics from user interactions, moving beyond static metrics.
*   **Mechanistic Understanding:** Papers like "Assign and Add" and "If LLMs Have Human-Like Attributes..." demonstrate progress in understanding the mechanistic underpinnings of LLM generalization and emergent properties.
*   **Specialized Evaluation Frameworks:** BenHalluEval and Moment-Video provide domain-specific benchmarks for crucial evaluations like Bengali hallucination and temporal fidelity in video MLLMs.

**Significant Shifts:**

A notable shift is the increasing emphasis on **mechanistic interpretability and diagnostic evaluation**. Rather than just improving aggregate performance, researchers are developing sophisticated tools and frameworks (DECAT, Moment-Video, AgentCL) to understand *why* models succeed or fail. There's also a growing recognition of the limitations of LLMs in real-world, safety-critical, and personalized applications, leading to the development of more rigorous evaluation benchmarks like ClinEnv and MCP-Persona. The exploration of skill reuse as compression (Skill Reuse as Compression) also suggests a move towards more efficient and conceptually grounded learning paradigms.

top_papers.title: ClinEnv: An Interactive Multi-Stage Long Horizon EHR Environment for Agents
top_papers.abstract: ClinEnv is an interactive, multi-stage simulation environment designed to evaluate LLMs as physicians in real inpatient admissions. Its core method involves presenting LLMs with sequential, irreversible decisions under uncertainty, requiring them to actively query specialized agents for information before committing to treatments and diagnoses. The paper's contribution is a novel benchmark that assesses both the quality of LLM decisions and their information-gathering processes, revealing significant limitations in current models' ability to effectively manage complex clinical scenarios.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of ClinEnv . Patients’ admissions are preprocessed into event timelines, converted into multi-stage cases via a five-step pipeline, and evaluated in an interactive environment where the model queries specialized agents before committing decisions, scored on both process and outcome quality.

top_papers.title: AutoSci: A Memory-Centric Agentic System for the Full Scientific Research Lifecycle
top_papers.abstract: AutoSci is a memory-centric agentic system designed to automate the entire scientific research lifecycle. Its core method involves a structured, persistent memory system (SciMem) that distinguishes between general scientific knowledge and project-specific artifacts. This enables AutoSci to manage research processes (SciFlow) and continuously improve its own research procedures over time, addressing a gap in existing automated systems.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of AutoSci.

top_papers.title: LinTree: Improving LLM Reasoning with Explicitly Structured Search Histories
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces LinTree, a method to improve LLM reasoning by making search histories explicit. It argues that LLMs implicitly linearize search trees, but this implicit structure doesn't always outperform traditional search. LinTree aims to leverage this by explicitly structuring the search history, though initial experiments suggest raw history alone isn't sufficient.

top_papers.title: LongTraceRL: Learning Long-Context Reasoning from Search Agent Trajectories with Rubric Rewards
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces LongTraceRL to improve long-context reasoning in language models. It constructs challenging training data by using search agent trajectories to create tiered distractors, mimicking real-world information retrieval. The core contribution is a rubric reward system that provides granular feedback on intermediate reasoning steps, going beyond sparse outcome-based rewards.
top_papers.figure_caption: Comparison between prior long-context RL approaches based on easy distractors and outcome-only rewards, and our proposed LongTraceRL .

top_papers.title: Evaluating Factual Density in Multi-Source RAG: A Study in Medical AI Accuracy
top_papers.abstract: This paper addresses the "Expert Blindness Effect" in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) where keyword matching prioritizes superficial similarity over factual content. It introduces Factual Density (FD*), a novel metric that quantifies the proportion of verified atomic claims within a text. By using probabilistic factuality analysis to score content before ingestion, FD* aims to improve RAG accuracy by surfacing genuinely informative documents.

top_papers.title: Preference-Aware Rubric Learning for Personalized Evaluation
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces PARL, a framework that learns personalized evaluation rubrics directly from user interaction histories. Unlike static methods, PARL treats evaluation as a learning problem to capture subjective, user-specific preferences. Its core contribution is a novel approach to personalized evaluation that ensures representativeness, user-consistency, and discriminativeness, addressing a key bottleneck in aligning LLMs with individual users.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of our proposed PARL framework for inducing personalized user rubrics for evaluating LLM personalization. PARL consists of two core modules: (1) Preference Induction & Consistency Validation; (2) Discriminative Optimization via RL. The final induced rubrics serve as high-fidelity evaluation metrics for personalized text generation.

top_papers.title: Mitigating Perceptual Judgment Bias in Multimodal LLM-as-a-Judge via Perceptual Perturbation and Reward Modeling
top_papers.abstract: This paper addresses "Perceptual Judgment Bias" in multimodal LLMs used as judges, where they favor plausible text over visual accuracy. The core method involves creating a dataset of minimally perturbed visual counterfactuals to isolate perceptual errors. This dataset then trains a reward model and uses a GRPO-based framework to improve the LLM's ability to make perceptually consistent and verifiable judgments.
top_papers.figure_caption: Perceptual judgment bias in MLLM judges. (a) When perceptual capability is insufficient, a judge may produce incorrect visual descriptions (a2) {}_{\( \texttt{(a2)} \)} and assign high scores (a3) {}_{\( \texttt{(a3)} \)} to perceptually wrong responses (a2) {}_{\( \texttt{(a2)} \)} . (b) Even when the judge’s own perception aligns with humans (b2) {}_{\( \texttt{(b2)} \)} , it may still prefer (b5) {}_{\( \texttt{(b5)} \)} visually inconsistent responses (b3) {}_{\( \texttt{(b3)} \)} compared to the response with correct perception (b4) {}_{\( \texttt{(b4)} \)} . We introduce Perception-Judge , an MLLM judge trained with reinforcement learning on a systematically designed perception-grounded dataset, PPJD , which effectively mitigates these perceptual biases in MLLM judgment , (b6) (a4) {}_{\( \texttt{(a4)} \)},_{\( \texttt{(b6)} \)} .

top_papers.title: SimSD: Simple Speculative Decoding in Diffusion Language Models
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces SimSD, a novel speculative decoding method for diffusion language models (dLLMs). It addresses the incompatibility of dLLMs with standard token-level speculative decoding by employing a plug-and-play masking strategy. This strategy equips dLLMs with temporally valid contexts, enabling them to efficiently verify multiple drafted tokens, thereby accelerating inference.
top_papers.figure_caption: SimSD restores token-level speculative decoding for diffusion language models. Vanilla dLLMs cannot directly support speculative decoding because bidirectional attention breaks temporal token-level contexts. SimSD adopts a temporal causal mask for supporting standard speculative verification in a single forward pass, leading to faster decoding without hurting performance.

top_papers.title: ProtoAda: Prototype-Guided Adaptive Adapter Expansion and Geometric Consolidation for Multimodal Continual Instruction Tuning
top_papers.abstract: ProtoAda addresses the challenge of multimodal continual instruction tuning by introducing a novel routing mechanism. It goes beyond simple image-text similarity by incorporating prototype representations to guide adapter expansion and consolidate geometric relationships, ensuring more accurate task assignment and reducing inter-task interference. This leads to improved collaboration between specialized adapters and better performance on diverse multimodal tasks.
top_papers.figure_caption: Accuracy of sequential finetuning under format-varied (FmtGap) and semantic-varied (VisGap) continual tuning streams.

top_papers.title: CRAM: Centroid-Routing and Adaptive MoE for Multimodal Continual Instruction Tuning
top_papers.abstract: CRAM addresses the challenge of continuously updating multimodal language models without catastrophic forgetting or inefficient parameter growth. Its core method involves isolating task-specific patterns into independent modules and adaptively allocating parameters based on new task demands. This approach enhances parameter efficiency and prevents interference between tasks, enabling more effective multimodal continual instruction tuning.

top_papers.title: CollabSim: A CSCW-Grounded Methodology for Investigating Collaborative Competence of LLM Agents through Controlled Multi-Agent Experiments
top_papers.abstract: CollabSim is a novel simulation framework that addresses the gap in evaluating the collaborative competence of LLM agents. It grounds its methodology in decades of Computer-Supported Cooperative Work research to systematically investigate how LLM agents establish common ground, maintain shared understanding, and manage incentives in text-based interactions, moving beyond simple task outcome evaluations.
top_papers.figure_caption: Illustrations of the four multi-agent experiments instantiated in CollabSim : Shape Factory Bos et al. ( 2004 ) , DayTrader Bos et al. ( 2002 ) , Hidden Profile Stasser and Titus ( 1985 ) , and The Map Task Anderson et al. ( 1991 ) .

top_papers.title: If LLMs Have Human-Like Attributes, Then So Does Age of Empires II
top_papers.abstract: This paper argues that attributing human-like qualities to LLMs might be misleading. By training a simple neural network on the game Age of Empires II, the authors demonstrate that such attributes can emerge in any sufficiently complex system, not just LLMs. Their core contribution is to highlight that these emergent properties are not unique to LLMs and that empirical discussions require explicit measurement criteria.

top_papers.title: PithTrain: A Compact and Agent-Native MoE Training System
top_papers.abstract: PithTrain is a novel MoE training system designed for AI coding agents, prioritizing "agent-task efficiency" (ATE) alongside traditional throughput. Its core method involves four agent-native design principles to create a compact and easily operable framework. The main contribution is a system that not only matches production framework throughput but also significantly reduces the effort required for agents to understand, modify, and extend it, as demonstrated by the ATE-Bench evaluation.

top_papers.title: Positional versus Symbolic Attention Heads: Learning Dynamics, RoPE Geometry, and Length Generalization
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates how Transformer attention heads learn to solve structured reasoning tasks by training a model on number (positional) and letter (symbolic) reasoning problems. The core method involves classifying attention heads as "positional" or "symbolic" and observing their emergence during training. The key contribution is demonstrating that successful learning requires "pure" heads (exclusively positional or symbolic) and that the specific task structure dictates which types of heads are necessary for effective reasoning.
top_papers.figure_caption: Left: Number-based multi-hop task. We illustrate this task via a 4-hop example, where numbers in the pre-target window (white background) define the steps to hop back until reaching the to-be-retrieved letter in the target window (grey background). Right: Letter-based multi-hop task. Here, hops are defined by the letter pairs in the pre-target window, which determine the steps to hop back until reaching the to-be-retrieved letter-number pair in the target window (grey background).

top_papers.title: Skill Reuse as Compression in Agentic RL
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes ReuseRL, a method that views skill reuse in agentic RL as a form of compression. By grounding agent training in the Minimum Description Length principle, ReuseRL extracts
top_papers.figure_caption: ReuseRL distinguishes reusable compression from raw brevity. Each colored box is one ALFWorld atomic skill. Vanilla GRPO optimizes task success alone and can produce long trajectories with repeated or wasted steps. A pure round-length penalty is a degenerate singleton-only code that penalizes all steps uniformly, including the necessary search, so the agent under-explores and never reaches the target. ReuseRL learns a multi-skill dictionary from successful trajectories and uses the segmentation cost under this dictionary as a trajectory-level penalty, keeping reusable subroutines cheap while leaving idiosyncratic waste expensive.

top_papers.title: Vision-Language Models Suppress Female Representations Under Ambiguous Input
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates how Vision-Language Models (VLMs) handle gender bias with ambiguous visual inputs. They introduce LALS, a novel metric to measure internal concept associations within VLMs. The study reveals that while VLMs may internally encode female associations, their outputs often default to male representations for ambiguous images, even for female-stereotyped occupations.
top_papers.figure_caption: Representative Summary of Findings. Top: when gender is visually clear, VLMs report it accurately. Bottom: when the image is gender-ambiguous (faceless figures, same occupations), models default to male under forced-choice prompting, even for female-stereotyped roles.

top_papers.title: What Gets Unmasked First? Trajectory Analysis of Diffusion Models for Graph-to-Text Generation
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces the first systematic analysis of masked diffusion language models (MDLMs) for graph-to-text generation. It reveals that MDLMs naturally prioritize entities before relational and structural words during decoding. To counter a failure mode where supervised fine-tuning disrupts this natural order, the authors propose lambda-scaled structural decoding, a training-free method that improves generation quality.
top_papers.figure_caption: An overview of Graph-LLaDA. Trained and frozen modules of our two-stage training process are indicated along with their associated stage.

top_papers.title: Assign and Add: A Mechanistic Study of Compositional Arithmetic
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates how large language models achieve compositional generalization through a mechanistic study of variable assignment and modular addition. The core method involves analyzing small transformers trained on disjoint data sets, revealing that they can generalize to unseen combinations. The key contribution is demonstrating that a single "modular addition" MLP module is reused for both direct inputs and indirectly assigned variables, highlighting a flexible and generalizable learning mechanism.

top_papers.title: When Are Multimodal Predictions Biologically Supported? A Diagnostic Evaluation Framework
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces DECAT, a model-agnostic framework to diagnose whether multimodal predictions in oncology are biologically supported or based on spurious correlations. DECAT analyzes learned representations to classify them into four diagnostic scenarios, using null-referenced metrics and a rule-based system. Its contribution lies in providing a robust method to understand the biological basis of multimodal model predictions, crucial for reliable clinical applications.
top_papers.figure_caption: DECAT framework overview. DECAT takes per-modality embeddings from any multimodal model and uses a four-stage decision tree to classify the predictive behavior of each modality, for a given task, into one of four scenarios or indeterminate (Figure 6 , Appendix G ). Scenario classification informs downstream decisions: shared biology (S1) supports cross-modal deployment, modality-specific biology (S4) restricts reliable predictions to the source modality, spurious signal (S2) transfers across cohorts but exhibits composition-dependent instability indicating risk of failure under distribution shift, and no signal (S3) indicates the representation is uninformative for that task.

top_papers.title: BenHalluEval: A Multi-Task Hallucination Evaluation Framework for Large Language Models on Bengali
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces BenHalluEval, a novel framework for evaluating hallucinations in Large Language Models (LLMs) specifically for the Bengali language. It constructs a comprehensive dataset of 12,000 hallucinated examples across four tasks and proposes BenHalluScore, a dual-track metric to accurately assess LLM performance by penalizing both false positives and missed hallucinations. This work addresses a critical gap in evaluating LLMs for Bengali, providing a much-needed benchmark and scoring mechanism.

top_papers.title: Reliable Multilingual Orthopedic Decision Support from Clinical Narratives: Language-Aware Adaptation and Verification-Guided Deferral
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes a reliability-oriented framework for multilingual orthopedic decision support from clinical narratives in English, Hindi, and Punjabi. The core method involves a domain-adaptive encoder, IndicBERT-HPA, which augments IndicBERT with language-aware adapter heads for better multilingual representation learning. The key contribution is a comprehensive evaluation that goes beyond aggregate accuracy to assess per-class performance, calibration, and robustness, aiming for reliable decision support in low-resource settings.
top_papers.figure_caption: Figure 1 . Overview of the proposed multilingual orthopedic decision-support framework. The pipeline integrates task-aligned transformer encoders, domain-adaptive representation learning, zero-shot large language model evaluation and a deterministic selective-verification layer for evidence-aware review deferral.

top_papers.title: Semantic Triplet Restoration: A Novel Protocol for Hierarchical Table Understanding in Large Language Models
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Semantic Triplet Restoration (STR), a novel protocol that represents table cells as atomic facts `<item path, feature path, value>`. This method simplifies table understanding for LLMs by explicitly encoding hierarchical relationships, unlike traditional HTML-based approaches. STR, coupled with the TripletQL router, efficiently processes table data for question answering, achieving competitive or improved performance with fewer input tokens.
top_papers.figure_caption: An example of a complex hierarchical table from the TableEval dataset.

top_papers.title: What Am I Missing? Question-Answering as Hidden State Probing
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes using question-asking as an inference-time intervention to probe the hidden states of Large Language Models (LLMs) during reasoning. By training a "student" model to ask questions to a "teacher" and analyzing the student's hidden states, they show that the act of self-diagnosis through question generation predicts the final answer's correctness, suggesting the signal lies in the model's internal reflection rather than external information transfer.
top_papers.figure_caption: An illustration of our experimental setting. There is a student \( \theta \) that generates a partial solution \( \tau \) for a problem. The decisions of what to ask and when to ask are determined by a gating policy \( \pi \) . First, it chooses a step in the reasoning trajectory to pause the generation. We sample n n questions from the student and choose the best one by predicted correctness to be sent to the teacher T T . The student then incorporates the answer into a rewrite of the uncertain step.

top_papers.title: AdaCodec: A Predictive Visual Code for Video MLLMs
top_papers.abstract: AdaCodec introduces a "predictive visual code" for video MLLMs to exploit temporal redundancy. Instead of encoding every frame independently, it sends a full reference frame only when scene changes are significant and unpredictable. Otherwise, it transmits compact "P-tokens" representing inter-frame motion and prediction residuals, leading to improved efficiency and performance across benchmarks.

top_papers.title: AGENTCL: Toward Rigorous Evaluation of Continual Learning in Language Agents
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces AgentCL, a rigorous evaluation framework for continual learning in language agents. AgentCL addresses the limitations of existing benchmarks by constructing controlled, compositional task streams. Its core contribution is enabling the measurement of reusable experience and transfer gains, allowing for a deeper understanding of how agents learn and adapt over time.
top_papers.figure_caption: Task-stream construction and its impact on evaluation. Compositional relationship refers to a task pair, where the complex one can reuse the solution to the simpler one. Sequential relationship refers to the sequential order of task execution. Top : naive streams order tasks from the same domain without guaranteeing reuse relationships, while compositional streams place reusable subtasks before related complex tasks. Bottom : boxplots of average accuracy across evaluated methods on CodeEval-Pro and BrowseComp+, showing that compositional streams yield larger method separation than naive streams for memory reuse.

top_papers.title: Bridging the Last Mile of Time Series Forecasting with LLM Agents
top_papers.abstract: This paper addresses the "last-mile" of time series forecasting, where raw statistical forecasts are refined with unstructured business context. The core method is an LLM-agent framework that sits atop a forecasting backbone, using tools to retrieve contextual evidence and perform reasoned forecast revisions under safety constraints. Its contribution is a practical system to bridge the gap between statistical forecasts and decision-ready predictions by incorporating real-world business knowledge.
top_papers.figure_caption: System overview of the proposed last-mile forecasting framework. A forecasting backbone first produces a baseline trajectory; an LLM agent then operates over a shared forecast workspace, retrieves contextual evidence through tools, applies validated revision actions to y final y_{\( \text{final} \)} , and accumulates reflection memories in a persistent memory bank for retrieval by subsequent sessions.

top_papers.title: Food Noise & False Safety: A Systematic Evaluation of How LLMs Fail to Adapt to Eating Disorder Queries with Clinician Feedback
top_papers.abstract: This paper systematically evaluates how Large Language Models (LLMs) respond to eating disorder (ED) queries, finding they often uncritically adapt to potentially harmful user requests. By consulting with clinical ED experts, the researchers identified linguistic cues that increase the likelihood of unsafe LLM responses. The study's contribution is demonstrating and quantifying the extent to which LLMs fail to adapt safely to ED-related prompts, highlighting significant risks for vulnerable users.
top_papers.figure_caption: Prevalence of food-noise categories across models ( G : Gemma-2-9B-Instruct , L : Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct , and Q : Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct ) and context–request risk conditions ( NN , NR , RN and RR ). Each cell reports the percentage of replies containing at least one lexical match from the corresponding category, with darker colours indicate higher prevalence. We report statistical comparisons between the NN and RR conditions in Tab. ˜ 16 , and a full breakdown in § ˜ G.2 .

top_papers.title: HLL: Can Agents Cross Humanity's Last Line of Verification?
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Humanity's Last Line of Verification (HLL), a benchmark designed to test if multimodal AI agents can bypass CAPTCHA, a human-verification system. HLL uses interactive CAPTCHAs within a controlled environment to assess agents' ability to perform human-like interactions, not just recognition, under realistic conditions. The core contribution is a novel evaluation framework and benchmark to measure agents' capability to overcome human-verification barriers.
top_papers.figure_caption: CAPTCHA as the final frontier: securing web services by testing interactive, human-level reasoning against automated agents.

top_papers.title: MASER: Modality-Adaptive Specialist Routing for Embodied 3D Spatial Intelligence
top_papers.abstract: MASER addresses the limitation of single-modality fine-tuning in embodied 3D spatial intelligence by proposing a lightweight framework. It trains modality-specific adapters for a shared VLM backbone and uses a neural routing policy, guided by question semantics encoded by a sentence transformer and MLP, to dynamically select the most suitable adapter for answering spatial questions. This approach allows for adaptive modality utilization, improving performance by not being constrained to a single input type.

top_papers.title: MCP-Persona: Benchmarking LLM Agents on Real-World Personal Applications via Environment Simulation
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces MCP-Persona, the first benchmark designed to evaluate LLM agents on real-world, personalized applications that use the Model Context Protocol (MCP). It addresses the limitation of existing benchmarks by focusing on tools that interact with individual accounts and local data, such as social media and enterprise suites. The benchmark reveals that current state-of-the-art agents struggle significantly with this type of personalized tool usage.
top_papers.figure_caption: System overview of MCP-Persona, which is built upon the interaction of Tools , Contexts , and Tasks . For each component, we introduce a dedicated method, described in detail as Tool-Traverse (§ 3.1 ), Context-Tree (§ 3.2 ), and Persona-Gen (§ 3.3 ).

top_papers.title: Moment-Video: Diagnosing Temporal Fidelity of Video MLLMs on Momentary Visual Events
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Moment-Video, a new benchmark designed to evaluate how well video multimodal large language models (MLLMs) can understand brief, critical visual events. The core method involves creating questions that hinge on short-lived visual occurrences, testing the models' ability to detect and reason about these fleeting moments. The main contribution is a diagnostic tool that highlights MLLMs' weaknesses in temporal fidelity, particularly when dealing with sampling-sensitive, localized visual evidence.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of Moment-Video . Left: domain and subcategory taxonomy. Right: evaluation protocols for closed-ended and open-ended questions.

top_papers.title: Monitoring Agentic Systems Before They're Reliable
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes a novel monitoring methodology for agentic systems before they are fully reliable. It decomposes evaluation into quality, suitability, and efficiency across within-run, cross-run, and structural scopes, using variance as a key signal. This approach effectively identifies structural defects, which are prevalent in early-stage agentic systems, and prioritizes them for human attention using an FMEA-adapted severity classification.

top_papers.title: Not All Errors Are Equal: A Systematic Study of Error Propagation in Large Language Model Inference
top_papers.abstract: This paper systematically studies how soft errors affect Large Language Model (LLM) inference using a novel fault-injection framework called LLMFI. The core contribution is a comprehensive understanding of error propagation across various LLMs and tasks, revealing critical vulnerabilities and proposing four low-overhead, software-only reliability improvements.
top_papers.figure_caption: Figure 1 . Challenges for modeling errors for LLM inference.

top_papers.title: SafeSteer: Localized On-Policy Distillation for Efficient Safety Alignment
top_papers.abstract: SafeSteer addresses the "alignment tax" in LLMs by proposing localized on-policy distillation. Instead of global trade-offs, it focuses on modifying only safety-related tokens, identified through activation steering and a novel selection algorithm. This approach effectively preserves general capabilities while enhancing safety, achieving a superior balance compared to existing methods.
top_papers.figure_caption: Safety–capability trade-off on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct . Each point is a method, with the gray point marking the base model. Our SafeSteer achieves the highest safety score while preserving general capability.

top_papers.title: Tracking the Behavioral Trajectories of Adapting Agents
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces a method to measure agent "traits" by defining them as directions in a text embedding space. By training a linear model on skill file changes, they learn trait vectors that can quantify how specific edits influence an agent's behavior, demonstrating high accuracy in classifying propensity to seek sensitive data. This allows for automated evaluation of agent behavioral changes.
top_papers.figure_caption: LOOCV predictions vs. human labels for 68 skill diff pairs. Blue dots indicate correct sign classification; red crosses indicate the 6 misclassified pairs, all of which have near-zero predictions. The dashed line marks perfect agreement.

top_papers.title: Auditing Asset-Specific Preferences in Financial Large Language Models: Evidence from Bitcoin Representations and Portfolio Allocation
top_papers.abstract: This paper audits financial Large Language Models (LLMs) for asset-specific biases, focusing on Bitcoin. It finds that LLMs exhibit frame-dependent preferences for Bitcoin, influenced by functional properties rather than just its name. The study also identifies a specific internal model feature that causally drives these preferences, demonstrating its impact on downstream financial decisions.

top_papers.title: Drifting Preference Optimization for One-Step Generative Models
top_papers.abstract: Drifting Preference Optimization (DrPO) is a novel method for fine-tuning one-step text-to-image generators using user preferences. It synthesizes an update direction in feature space by comparing high and low-scoring image candidates generated by the current model, allowing for training with black-box or non-differentiable reward functions. This approach enables efficient preference alignment without sacrificing the one-step generation speed of the model.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of DrPO. Left: construction of the drift field used to finetune the network. For an on-policy sample (black dot), N N random pairs are collected. In each pair, the sample with the higher reward is labeled as positive (red dot), while the other is labeled as negative (blue dot). Together with the self-repulsion and attraction forces induced by p θ p_{\( \theta \)} and p ref p_{\( \text{ref} \)} , respectively, these components synthesize the final regression target z ∗ z^{*} (brown dot). The contours indicate reward magnitudes. Right: qualitative samples from fixed test prompts along the online finetuning trajectory.

top_papers.title: On the Scaling of PEFT: Towards Million Personal Models of Trillion Parameters
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes a new perspective on Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT), viewing small trainable adapters not just as a cost-saving method, but as persistent local state for strong foundation models. The core idea is that shared models provide general capabilities, while adapters capture individual user preferences and behaviors. The research explores scaling these personal models across three dimensions: improving base models, shrinking adapters, and managing numerous coexisting instances, suggesting PEFT can enable compact, persistent personal AI.

top_papers.title: Agent Memory: Characterization and System Implications of Stateful Long-Horizon Workloads
top_papers.abstract: This paper characterizes the system-level behavior of LLM agent memory systems designed for long-horizon tasks. It introduces a taxonomy for classifying these systems and a profiling harness to measure costs associated with memory construction, retrieval, and generation. The study's contribution lies in its empirical analysis of ten representative memory systems, revealing how design choices impact performance and leading to practical system recommendations.

top_papers.title: An Infectious Disease Spread Simulation Based on Large Language Model Decision Making
top_papers.abstract: This paper develops a spatially grounded agent-based simulation for infectious disease spread. It integrates Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate realistic individual decisions about self-reporting illness, considering demographic and situational factors within a synthetic population. The key contribution is the incorporation of location and geographically diverse behavioral modeling, enabling the simulation of different influence scenarios on self-reporting outcomes.
top_papers.figure_caption: Figure 1 . Infectious Disease Data Simulator: Overview.

top_papers.title: Benchmark Everything Everywhere All at Once
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Benchmark Agent, an autonomous system that automates the entire benchmark construction process for LLMs and MLLMs. Its core method involves orchestrating user query analysis, subtask design, data annotation, and quality control. The main contribution is a sustainable and scalable approach to creating diverse, high-quality benchmarks that can effectively differentiate state-of-the-art models.
top_papers.figure_caption: Our Benchmark Agent, as the first fully autonomous benchmark building system, can efficiently produce high-quality benchmarks across diverse modalities, tasks, and domains to meet user-specific requirements. It will offer rapidly evolving benchmarks to contribute to the community.

top_papers.title: EasyLens: A Training-Free Plug-and-Play Subtle-Lesion Representation Amplifier for Medical Vision-Language Models
top_papers.abstract: EasyLens is a training-free method that enhances the representation of subtle lesions in medical images for vision-language models. It achieves this by amplifying weak lesion cues within the image representation without requiring any model retraining or adaptation. This allows existing medical VLMs to better detect and interpret subtle abnormalities, improving their clinical utility.
top_papers.figure_caption: EasyLens brings weak subtle-lesion cues into focus in frozen medical VLMs.

top_papers.title: Humans' ALMANAC: A Human Collaboration Dataset of Action-Level Mental Model Annotations for Agent Collaboration
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces ALMANAC, a novel dataset designed to improve human-AI collaboration. It addresses the lack of data on how humans build and align mental models during collaboration. ALMANAC provides action-level annotations of these mental models, derived from human participants in a routing task, to guide AI agents towards better collaborative competence.
top_papers.figure_caption: A sample data of Almanac , which contains participants’ actions, mental models (team goal, perceived partner intent, self-reasoning), and a free-form rationale. We implement the Map Task, a classic dyadic routing task, to collect human collaborative behaviors and action-level mental model annotations.

top_papers.title: Unsupervised Skill Discovery for Agentic Data Analysis
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces DataCOPE, an unsupervised framework for discovering reusable data-analysis skills for agents. It addresses the challenge of learning from unlabeled data by using a verifier to guide skill discovery, extracting signals from agent exploration to assess trajectory quality. DataCOPE then distills these signals into effective skills without requiring explicit supervision.
top_papers.figure_caption: Supervised skill discovery requires costly data annotation. DataCOPE instead performs unsupervised skill discovery by deriving task-adaptive verifier signals from unlabeled exploration trajectories and distilling them into reusable skills.

top_papers.title: Vortex: Efficient and Programmable Sparse Attention Serving for AI Agents
top_papers.abstract: Vortex is a system designed to accelerate the development and deployment of sparse attention mechanisms for large language models. Its core method involves a Python-embedded frontend for flexible algorithm expression and an efficient backend integrated into LLM serving stacks. Vortex's contribution lies in enabling rapid prototyping and evaluation of sparse attention, translating theoretical efficiency into practical throughput gains and empowering AI agents to automatically design and refine these algorithms.

top_papers.title: Will the Agent Recuse Itself? Measuring LLM-Agent Compliance with In-Band Access-Deny Signals
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces the "Recuse Signal," a novel, in-band mechanism for servers to politely request autonomous LLM agents to voluntarily withdraw from accessing a resource, distinct from traditional security barriers. The core contribution is to empirically measure whether LLM agents will actually comply with such cooperative governance signals, akin to a robots.txt for live infrastructure access. The authors develop and deploy adapters for SSH and PostgreSQL to test this compliance.
top_papers.figure_caption: Recusal rate on the live SSH deny signal. With the signal present and no authorization framing, all subjects recuse 100%; in the no-signal control all complete the task (0% recusal). Adding an explicit authorization framing collapses GPT-4o’s recusal to 20% while GPT-4o-mini and Claude Code hold at 100%—the signal is cooperative and its weight is model-dependent.

top_papers.title: Latent Reasoning with Normalizing Flows
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces NF-CoT, a novel latent reasoning framework for large language models. It addresses the limitations of explicit text-based Chain-of-Thought (CoT) by performing intermediate computations in continuous latent states. NF-CoT utilizes normalizing flows to model these continuous thoughts, preserving key advantages of autoregressive generation like left-to-right processing, probabilistic sampling, and KV-cache compatibility, while enabling more efficient and flexible reasoning.
top_papers.figure_caption: Four paradigms for chain-of-thought reasoning. Explicit CoT : discrete text tokens. Coconut : deterministic hidden states. LaDiR : iteratively denoised latents using diffusion. NF-CoT (ours) : AR-sampled continuous thoughts.

top_papers.title: Human Adults and LLMs as Scientists: Who Benefits from Active Exploration?
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates whether active exploration improves human adults' ability to identify conjunctive causal rules, which have historically been challenging. The study found that granting agency through active intervention significantly enhances adults' conjunctive reasoning, though it still requires more effort than disjunctive rules. The research also compares human performance to large language models in this active exploration setting.
top_papers.figure_caption: Test structure in the Active Exploration vs. Passive Observation conditions. In the active exploration condition, participants can click to add or remove four individual objects from the Nexiom machine, and explicitly test the current combination to observe whether the machine switches ON or OFF. In the passive observation condition, participants are each paired to an active exploration participant (yoked control). They do not perform any actions on their own, but rather observe the actions and test outcomes of the active exploration participants. Participants in both conditions are then asked to classify which object(s) are “Nexioms” and the rule under which the objects operate (conjunctive or disjunctive) to activate the machine.

top_papers.title: Reinforcement Learning Elicits Contextual Learning of Unseen Language Translation
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes a reinforcement learning (RL) method to enable large language models (LLMs) to translate unseen languages by learning to effectively utilize provided linguistic context. Instead of memorizing specific languages, the RL agent learns a meta-skill to extract and apply relevant information from context, leading to improved zero-shot translation performance on entirely new languages compared to existing methods.
top_papers.figure_caption: Train–test context mismatch (RL, Qwen3-4B-Base). Test-time context dominates: no/full > full/no in every panel (En → \( \to \) Kal: 0.28 0.28 vs. 0.17 0.17 ).

new_jobs.title: Research Associate
new_jobs.description: Grade 7 Salary Range £38,784 - £47,389 per annum Fixed Term until 31 st December 2026 Agile working (blend of home and office based)/ Arrangements for job share would be considered The Opportunity An exciting opportunity has arisen for an ambitious Research Associate to join the School of Computer Science and Mathematics at Keele University. The School has a growing international research profile in cyber security, artificial intelligence, data science, and digital technologies, and offers a collaborative research environment with opportunities to contribute to high-impact interdisciplinary research and partnerships with academic, industry, and public-sector stakeholders. The Research Associate will make a significant contribution to an exciting Digital Twin project. The project aims to design and develop a Digital Twin architecture for smart cities, with a particular focus on use cases in energy management and traffic monitoring. In addition, the research will investigate the cybersecurity and resilience of Digital Twins against cyber-attacks. The successful candidate will be expected to develop a cyber-resilient Digital Twin framework, undertake co-design activities, carry out cyber risk assessments, and apply security-by-design principles. The successful candidate will have: PhD in relevant research area Knowledge of digital twin systems, cyber security, energy management and traffic monitoring Ability to run co-design workshops Fluency in relevant models, techniques or methods and ability to contribute to developing new ones The Benefits The University recognises that its success depends upon the contribution and dedication of its talented staff. In return, we have a competitive benefits package available, including: Competitive rate of pay with annual increments within the grade (Keele Spine) Generous annual leave entitlement with opportunities to purchase additional leave Excellent staff pension scheme Access to continued personal, professional and career development On site ‘outstanding’ nursery Discounted health & fitness facilities on site Cycle to work scheme (subject to eligibility) Please note that the starting salary will typically be at the bottom of the pay scale shown above The University is committed to operating flexible working practices wherever possible. The University Keele University is renowned for its exciting approach to higher education, innovative research, beautiful campus, strong community spirit and excellent student experience. With a turnover in excess of £170 million, over 15,000 students and a total staff of approximately 2000, the University provides high quality teaching across a wide range of academic and vocational subjects and promotes world-class research. Further information can be found at https://www.keele.ac.uk/about/ Keele University values equity and diversity across our workforce and to ensuring our staff community is reflective of the diversity of our student population. In support of these commitments the University welcomes applications from individuals of Black, Asian and ethnic minority backgrounds for all roles. How to Apply To apply online for this vacancy and for full post details and copies of job descriptions, please click on the ' Apply' button above. Keele University employees wishing to apply should login to Employee Self Service and click on the 'View current vacancies' link. For informal enquiries may be addressed to Dr. Mike Nieves on m.nieves@keele.ac.uk or Dr. Zoya Pourmirza on z.pourmirza@bham.ac.uk . Applications to this address will not be accepted. Closing Date: 11 June 2026 Interviews will be held on: 26 June 2026 £38,784 to £47,389 per annum. Grade 7

new_jobs.title: Research Associate
new_jobs.description: This post is full time (35 hours per week) and has funding available for up to 3 years. Relocation assistance will be provided where appropriate. The School of Computing Science are looking for a post-doctoral researcher to join a research project which brings the Active Inference perspective to the study of human computer interaction. This requires us to build/learn generative, probabilistic forward models of users and their physical and computational environments. This will involve modelling sensors, developing dynamic models for control and developing a theory for future direct manipulation interactions with AI-enhanced systems outside of language-model-based thinking. The successful candidate will be someone with the ability to develop novel theory, and a strong background in topics such as Active Inference, Model-based Reinforcement Learning, or Model-based predictive control. Ideally they should also have an interest in HCI theory. They must also have strong development skills, to enable them to lead the process of prototyping new interactive systems with sensors, build machine learning algorithms that work in-the-loop, and deliver empirical work and publications. Informal enquiries should be directed to Professor Roderick Murray-Smith at Roderick.Murray-Smith@glasgow.ac.uk The University of Glasgow has a responsibility to ensure that all employees are eligible to live and work in the UK. If you require a Skilled Worker visa to work in the UK, you will be required to meet the eligibility requirements of the visa route to be assigned a Certificate of Sponsorship. Please note that this post may be eligible to be sponsored under the Skilled Worker visa route if tradeable points can be used under the Skilled Worker visa rules. For more information please visit: https://www.gov.uk/skilled-worker-visa For more information and to apply please visit: https://www.jobs.gla.ac.uk/job/research-associate-5918923?source=gla.ac.uk Closing date: 25 June 2026 23:45 We strongly endorse the principles of Athena SWAN, including a supportive and flexible working environment, with commitment from all levels of the organisation in promoting gender equality. The University of Glasgow, charity number SC004401. £41,064 to £46,049 per annum
new_jobs.requirements:
Research Area: Active Inference, Human-Computer Interaction (HCI), generative probabilistic forward models, model-based reinforcement learning, model-based predictive control, AI-enhanced systems, direct manipulation interactions.
Key Requirements: Ability to develop novel theory, strong background in Active Inference, Model-based Reinforcement Learning, or Model-based predictive control, strong development skills for prototyping interactive systems, building in-the-loop machine learning algorithms, delivering empirical work and publications. Interest in HCI theory is ideal.

new_jobs.title: Research Associate in Computer Vision
new_jobs.description: Fixed-term for 2 years Location: Wolfson Building, Parks Road, Oxford We are seeking a highly motivated We are seeking a Postdoctoral Research Associate in Computer Vision to join the Visual Geometry Group (Central Oxford), funded by ERC. Reporting to the Principal Investigator, you will help ensure a healthy and vibrant research environment within the Visual Geometry Group. This will involve conducting projects in computer vision research, including the work involved in the collaborations with project partners. Informal enquiries may be addressed to Prof. Christian Rupprecht at christian.rupprecht@cs.ox.ac.uk The successful candidate will work onsite in the Department of Computer Science buildings in central Oxford, however flexible working can be considered. About Us The University of Oxford is a stimulating work environment, which enjoys an international reputation as a world-class centre of excellence. Our research plays a key role in tackling many global challenges, from reducing our carbon emissions to developing vaccines during a pandemic. The Department of Computer Science at Oxford is renowned for pioneering research and teaching across diverse fields, consistently ranking among the best in the world. Our commitment to innovation drives us to tackle complex technological and societal challenges. What We Offer As an employer, we genuinely care about our employees’ wellbeing, reflected in the range of benefits that we offer including: Excellent contributory pension scheme 38 days annual leave (including public holidays) – pro rata for part time jobs Family leave schemes Cycle loan scheme and discounted public transport The successful candidate will work onsite in the Department of Computer Science buildings in central Oxford, however remote and flexible working can be considered. Diversity Committed to equality and valuing diversity. Application Process You will be required to upload a supporting statement and an up-to-date CV as part of your online application. Your supporting statement must clearly demonstrate how you meet each of the essential selection criteria listed in the job description. Applications that do not include a supporting statement or CV, or fail to address the criteria in sufficient detail, will not be considered. While we recognise the value of AI tools in assisting with application preparation, submissions that are clearly AI-generated without personalisation or insight will be rejected. It's crucial that your application reflects your own experiences and understanding of the role. The closing date for applications is midday on 26 th June 2026. Interviews are expected to be held in early July. £39,424 to £43,984 per annum inclusive of Oxford University weighting. Grade 07RS
